City Council Approves Long-Delayed Bridge Repair Budget

By Sam Reporter, City Hall Correspondent — October 22, 2024

After three years of contentious debate, the city council voted unanimously on Monday to approve a forty-two million dollar repair budget for the Riverside Bridge, ending a stalemate that had become a fixture of local politics. The vote, taken just before midnight, came after a four-hour public comment session in which dozens of residents — many of whom commute over the bridge daily — urged the council to act.

The bridge, built in 1962 and last refurbished in 1998, has been restricted to passenger vehicles since a 2021 engineering survey found that two of its three main support beams had corroded beyond the threshold considered safe for trucks and buses. The restriction has rerouted commercial traffic through the downtown core, adding an estimated eight minutes to the average delivery route and contributing, business owners say, to a noticeable decline in foot traffic.

Council member Maria Alvarez, who has championed the repair since her election in 2022, called the vote "a long-overdue victory for common sense." Speaking after the meeting, she emphasised that the funding package includes provisions for ongoing maintenance — something the original 1998 refurbishment notably lacked. "We are not going to find ourselves back here in 2050 asking the same question," she said.

The cost will be split between the city's capital improvement fund (twenty-eight million dollars) and a state infrastructure grant (fourteen million dollars) that the council had been working to secure since spring. Construction is expected to begin in early 2025 and to last approximately eighteen months. During that period, the bridge will be closed entirely; the city has committed to running additional shuttle bus service across the alternate downtown route.

Not every council member came to the vote enthusiastically. Council member David Chen, who had previously argued for a cheaper partial-repair option, said he ultimately supported the full package because "doing this twice would cost more than doing it right once." He cautioned, however, that the city would need to "watch the change orders like a hawk" once construction begins.

The Riverside Bridge connects the east and west sides of the city across the Riverside River. Approximately twenty-two thousand vehicles cross it each day, according to the most recent traffic survey, making it the second-busiest river crossing in the metropolitan area.
